description: Suppose a general claim assigns a property to every case in its stated scope. A case within that scope that lacks the property is a 'phiwheki'; it shows that the claim is false as stated. A surprising case outside the scope is not a counterexample.
articulatory_notes: Bring the lips close together for unvoiced bilabial 'ph' and release the narrow breath into high-front unrounded 'i'. Keep the lips rounded and raise the back of the tongue for voiceless 'wh', then open into stressed middle-height front 'e'. Close the back of the tongue against the soft palate for unvoiced 'k' and finish with high-front unrounded 'i'.
sound_symbolism: The opening 'phi' recalls 'phisuwa' (example). Rounded, breathy 'whe' widens the middle before 'ki' closes at the soft palate and narrows into high 'i', as though a broad statement had met one exact case.
usage_notes: 'phiwheki' is a countable noun. The targeted claim may stand before it as possessor: 'sherewa phiwheki' (a counterexample to the claim). An ordinary 'phisuwa' (example) illustrates; a 'phiwheki' tests a universal claim and shows that it needs revision.

pillars.secular-buddhist-philosophy: One encountered case may loosen attachment to an overgeneralised view and invite a more conditional account.
pillars.peace-linguistics-practices: A counterexample directs attention to the claim's scope and the evidence within it, rather than to the standing of the person who spoke.

semantic_domains.cognition: a scoped case that disproves a general claim as stated
semantic_domains.communication: a concrete way to challenge overgeneralisation
semantic_domains.wisdom: attention to particulars that require a broad rule to be revised
